title: Wedding fruit cake
subtitle: Dark, dense traditional fruit cake for celebration tiers
excerpt: A rich, dark fruit cake made with dried vine fruits soaked in brandy, molasses, and warming spices, baked very slowly at a low temperature until deeply set and fragrant. The kind used under royal icing on celebration tiers: dense enough to support marzipan and icing without crumbling, with a long shelf life. Feed with additional brandy in the weeks before use. The recipe scales for different tin sizes.
sourceNotes: Rich fruit cake of the kind used for weddings, christenings, and Christmas is documented in British domestic cookery from the eighteenth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, freely available at Project Gutenberg), gives detailed recipes for rich fruit cake and wedding cake. The combination of vine fruits, mixed spice, ground almonds, and brandy-feeding is described as the standard method for celebration-tier fruit cakes in British baking manuals published before 1928.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 round cake 23 cm (serves 20 to 25 cut in small portions)
recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the fully cooled cake tightly in double greaseproof and foil. Freeze for up to 12 months. Defrost completely before applying marzipan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Soak the fruit overnight before baking. Once baked, wrap and feed with 2 tablespoons of brandy every 1 to 2 weeks for up to 3 months before covering with marzipan and royal icing.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 130°C. Very low and slow to allow the dense, fruit-heavy batter to set through without the exterior burning. Line the tin with a double layer of brown paper on the outside as further insulation.

recipeTools.notes: Deep-sided tin, at least 7 cm. Line base and sides with a double layer of baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: Poking holes in the surface of a cooled fruit cake with a skewer and spooning over a small amount of brandy, rum, or sherry so it soaks down into the crumb. Done every 1 to 2 weeks in the maturation period before the cake is covered and served. Feeding preserves the cake, deepens the flavour, and keeps the crumb moist. Use 2 tablespoons of spirit each time: more than this makes the cake soggy rather than moist. Always wrap the fed cake back in greaseproof and foil between feedings.

content.text: A rich fruit cake is one of the most forgiving cakes to bake: it is almost impossible to overcreate if the oven is low and the tin is well-lined. The work is in the preparation: soaking the fruit, lining the tin carefully, and giving the cake the
over the weeks before it is needed. A cake made 6 to 8 weeks ahead will always be better than one baked the day before.
Combine the currants, sultanas, raisins, glace cherries, and mixed peel in a large bowl. Pour over the brandy, stir, cover, and leave overnight. The fruit will absorb most of the liquid.
Prepare the tin and mix the batter

Heat the oven to 150°C conventional (130°C fan). Line the base and sides of a deep 23 cm round tin with a double layer of baking paper; tie a double band of brown paper around the outside of the tin to insulate it. Beat the butter and dark brown sugar together until pale and creamy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Stir in the treacle and golden syrup. Fold in the flour, ground almonds, mixed spice, and baking powder, then fold in the soaked fruit with any remaining brandy in the bowl.

Spoon the batter into the prepared tin and level the surface. Make a slight depression in the centre with the back of a spoon (this counteracts the rise and keeps the surface flat for icing). Bake for 3 to 3 hours 30 minutes, checking with a skewer from 2 hours 45 minutes. The cake is done when the skewer comes out clean and the top is deep brown. Leave to cool completely in the tin, then remove, wrap in greaseproof paper and foil, and store in a cool place. Feed with 2 tablespoons of brandy every 1 to 2 weeks.
